kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Latest OFFICIAL (Wed 08-Jul, Westmetall): $3,141.00 vs 07-Jul $3,119.00 = +$22.00 (+0.71%) — a FOURTH straight up-day off the $3,077 (.50) base, printed against US retaliatory strikes on Iran and a +5.2% Brent settle. Thursday 09-Jul officials post ~13:20 London. Still ~18.5% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 08-Jul official $3,147.00 vs 07-Jul $3,127.50 (+$19.50, +0.62%); contango narrowed to -$6.00. Four consecutive official gains — but the evening close was a doji at $3,138.5 into MA10 (~$3,145): $3,260 (.382) upgrades the count; $3,050 re-opens the downside.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 08-Jul official 290,825 t (Westmetall) — a further -1,600 t draw to below 291kt, extending the unbroken 2026 slide (509kt on 02-Jan → 291kt); ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.2 days of global output. Continuous draws through the correction and the rebound keep the structural-deficit signal intact.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Latest OFFICIAL Wed 08-Jul (Westmetall): $3,141.00 vs 07-Jul $3,119.00 = +$22.00 (+0.71%) — a FOURTH consecutive gain, the longest run since the 02-Jun top, priced against US retaliatory strikes on Iran and Brent's +5.2% settle. Thursday 09-Jul officials post ~13:20 London. ~18.5% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 08-Jul official $3,147.00 vs 07-Jul $3,127.50 (+$19.50, +0.62%); in the evening session the 3M traded $3,125.5–3,165 and the Closing Price printed a doji at $3,138.5 (-0.02%, SMM/LME) into MA10 resistance (~$3,145). Four straight official gains now stand between the $3,077 base and the $3,260 (.382) upgrade trigger. avg = June-2026 mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 08-Jul official: cash $3,141.00 vs 3M $3,147.00 = -$6.00 = shallow CONTANGO, narrowing from -$8.50 — the front end keeps tightening as stocks draw (290,825 t). No acute squeeze signal, but a flip back toward backwardation would flag the war-premium regime returning; watch nearby spreads while Gulf risk runs two-way (attacks 06–07 Jul, US strikes 08-Jul).

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: Most-traded SHFE aluminium settled 08-Jul at ¥23,075 (+0.68%, 2608 contract) and eased to ¥23,020 (-0.24%) in the night session ≈ $3,014/t excl-VAT at USD/CNY ~6.775 (SMM 09-Jul). Destocking at a four-year-high pace supports the bottom; the LME 3M ($3,147) still sits well above SHFE parity — import window shut. avg = June-2026 mean.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t and now ASSESSED by S&P Global Platts (06-Jul) — the highest quarterly Japan premium in a DECADE; +12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353 (settlements 03-Jul, Rio Tinto/South32; Bloomberg via Mining.com). War disruption to Gulf flows drove the reset. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: SMM Japan MJP spot premium printed $380/t at end-June (+123.5% vs end-2025) — the latest public spot print at run time — converging to the $395 Q3 settlement now Platts-assessed (06-Jul). Q4 negotiations (from ~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: FRESH Platts print: daily P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam assessed $525–560/t (midpoint $542.50) on 03-Jul — DOWN $70 vs the 13-May range ($600–625) as June's war-spike unwinds (S&P Global). NOTE: our prior reference ($470) was SMM's assessment — PRA bases differ; the change reflects the source upgrade, not a market move. The 6% duty + CBAM + Mozal's care-and-maintenance keep the structural floor.

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs dp Rotterdam — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (late-Jun; no fresh public print at run time); conversion premia stay stickier than ingot premia, and South32's Mozal (~580ktpa) on care & maintenance since 15-Mar-2026 supports European billet tightness — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: FRESH Platts print: US MW Transaction premium assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t on 03-Jul — DOWN 10.35¢ from 119.05¢ (20-May) as the LME correction mechanically deflates the 50% Section-232 tariff component (tariff-$ ≈ $1,570 at the 08-Jul cash $3,141). NOTE: prior dashboard reference (~$2,435) was SMM's US MW DDP print — bases differ (S&P Global).

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: Fastmarkets MB-AL-0020 ddp Midwest — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (~$0.20–0.22/lb market premium + 50% tariff; no fresh public MB print at run time). Platts' 03-Jul MW at 108.7¢/lb (≈$2,396) is the freshest US benchmark; the tariff-$ leg falls/rises mechanically with the LME — verify PRA.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY held above 101 on Wednesday near one-week highs — safe-haven bid on the US strikes plus a hawkish FOMC-minutes read (divided committee; forward guidance being de-emphasised under Chair Warsh). June-payrolls softness caps the upside (Trading Economics/Investing.com).

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Pair pinned on the 1.14 handle with DXY above 101; no fresh London-close print at run time — re-validate at the US open (Trading Economics).

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Offshore yuan ~6.77–6.78. China June CPI missed at +1.0% y/y while PPI accelerated to +4.1% — the strongest since Jul-2022, energy-driven; soft demand + hot costs keep policy easing in play (CNBC/NBS, released 09-Jul).

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen weak: ~¥162.5 (June London close 162.53 — NBC monthly FX); intervention risk persists — marginal premium support for Japan-bound cargoes.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: Jumped to 4.59% Wednesday — the highest since mid-May, after +8bp Tuesday — as the oil spike re-fuels inflation/tightening concern; FOMC June minutes showed a divided, hawkish committee. CME FedWatch (via SMM 09-Jul): July hold 69.0%; September cumulative-hike odds ~69% (51.9% +25bp, 17.0% +50bp).

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Brent settled $78.02 (+5.2%) on 08-Jul — the sharpest one-day jump since the war began — after US retaliatory strikes on Iran and the revocation of Iran's oil-sale waiver; OPEC+ spare capacity caps but no longer contains the move (CNBC).

macro.name: WTI crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: WTI settled $73.52 (+4.4%) on 08-Jul as the war premium re-priced; energy is the inflation channel the Fed minutes flag (CNBC).

macro.name: Natural gas ($/MMBtu)
macro.note: US Henry Hub ~$3.28 on record LNG feedgas; EU TTF spiked +8.2% to €47.97/MWh on 07-Jul after the Qatari LNG-carrier attack — no fresh public TTF print at run time; gas-linked smelter power costs remain the transmission channel (Reuters/TE).

macro_events: FOURTH STRAIGHT UP-DAY: Wednesday's 08-Jul officials posted $3,141.00 cash / $3,147.00 3M (+0.71/+0.62%); stock drew a further 1,600 t to 290,825 t (below 291kt); contango narrowed to -$6.00. The evening close printed a doji $3,138.5 into MA10 (~$3,145) — Thursday officials post ~13:20 London (Westmetall/SMM/LME).

US STRIKES IRAN (08-Jul): dozens of Iranian targets hit in retaliation for the 06–07 Jul attacks on three vessels; Iran's oil-sale waiver revoked; Trump — Iran will be hit 'very hard' — then framed the exchange as short of a long-term campaign. Indirect technical talks (fund returns, strait security) continue (CBS/ms.now/SMM).

OIL & RATES SHOCK: Brent settled $78.02 (+5.2%) and WTI $73.52 (+4.4%) on 08-Jul — the sharpest jump since the war began; US 10Y 4.59% (mid-May high); DXY >101; FOMC June minutes divided-hawkish. CME FedWatch: July hold 69%; September cumulative-hike odds ~69% — hike risk re-priced UP (CNBC/TE/CME via SMM).

CHINA JUNE CPI/PPI (released TODAY): CPI +1.0% y/y (miss vs ~1.1–1.2%; prior 1.2%; -0.3% m/m); PPI +4.1% y/y — the strongest since Jul-2022, energy-driven. Soft demand, hot costs — policy easing stays in play (CNBC/NBS, 09-Jul).

CHINA DESTOCKING ACCELERATES: warehouse withdrawals at a four-year high; ingot inventory 1.078 Mt Thursday (-20kt vs Mon, -52kt w/w); secondary-alloy ingots -9kt (6th straight weekly draw); A00 ¥23,040 (+100); SHFE settled ¥23,075 (+0.68%), night ¥23,020 (-0.24%) — import window shut (SMM 09-Jul).

PREMIUM MARKS RESET: Platts assessed Q3 MJP at a DECADE-HIGH $395/t (06-Jul); US MW eased to 108.7¢/lb (03-Jul, -10.35¢ vs 20-May) and EDU Rotterdam to $525–560 (-$70 vs 13-May) — regional spot normalising off the June spike while Japan's quarterly resets structurally up (S&P Global Platts/IndexBox).

FREIGHT RISING: BDI 2,875 (Tue 07-Jul, +2.8%) — highest since 08-Jun; Capesize 4,514, a sixth straight gain — dry-bulk legs for bauxite/alumina firming while Gulf war-risk surcharges stay bid (Baltic Exchange via Baird Maritime).

MACRO WEEK AHEAD: US June CPI mid-month; China June trade/aluminium exports ~mid-Jul; next PRA premium prints; Alcoa Q2 16-Jul (sector kickoff), Rio Q2 production mid-Jul, Hydro Q2 22-Jul (Trading Economics/company schedules).
lme_commentary: Fourth consecutive official gain: 08-Jul officials printed cash $3,141.00 (+0.71%) / 3M $3,147.00 (+0.62%) — the longest winning run since the 02-Jun top — with stock down another 1,600 t to 290,825 t and the contango narrowing to -$6.00. The evening session told a more cautious story: the 3M opened $3,140, tagged $3,165, faded to a doji close at $3,138.5 (-0.02%) into MA10 resistance (~$3,145), with daily bearish MACD momentum near exhaustion (histogram -3.7) and a slight short build (+2,151 lots OI). Dollar strength (DXY >101, 10Y 4.59%) and the strike/talks two-way capped what a +5.2% Brent settle would otherwise have delivered. The map is unchanged: $3,260 (.382) upgrades the recovery; a daily close below $3,050 re-opens $2,990–3,020 (Westmetall; SMM 09-Jul).

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference (early-Jul; no fresh public print at run time); LME's Platts-settled alumina contract closed $330 (02-Jul, Alcircle); oversupply persists; Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 — verify SMM/Fastmarkets

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$304 — easing on the building surplus (latest public reference) — verify Platts

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Settled ¥2,716 (+0.37%) on 08-Jul — a low-level short-covering bounce; price below ALL moving averages with the bearish alignment intact; port stocks building on high ex-China arrivals; SMM expects weak consolidation (SMM Aluminum Briefing, 08-Jul)

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309.4 — CME/TradingView (unchanged print at run time)

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$340/t, latest public reference) softer on oversupply vs Platts API region (~$304/t; Platts FOB futures ~$309.4; LME Platts-settled close $330 on 02-Jul); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026. SMM (08-Jul): China alumina inventory edged down WoW only on northern refinery maintenance; port stocks are BUILDING on high ex-China arrivals and the draw is expected to flip to buildup next week — the oversupply pattern is unchanged.

Guinea is the swing factor: the bauxite quota policy is still pending — SMM calls it the market's only clear bullish catalyst — while the Guinea–EGA settlement (GAC assets transferred to state-owned Nimba Mining; CBG supply resumed on commercial terms) normalises existing flows and the >$100/dmt export-price push remains the cost-side risk (SMM/Mining.com/Alcircle).

Gulf logistics risk stays on the board: ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz — the 06–08 Jul attack/strike cycle keeps a logistics risk premium on Gulf-bound alumina and caustic flows even as transit continues.
net_read: BASE HOLDING, TAPE TWO-WAY. Aluminium corrected ~19% from the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855) to the $3,061.5 C-wave low (02-Jul) at the .50 retracement, and has now risen FOUR straight official sessions to $3,141 cash / $3,147 3M (08-Jul) with LME stock below 291kt and the contango narrowing to -$6. The geopolitical tape is explicitly two-way: Iran hit three vessels (06–07 Jul), the US answered with strikes on dozens of targets and revoked the oil waiver (08-Jul; Brent +5.2%) — yet technical talks continue and Washington signals no long-term campaign. Physically the market stays tight-side: Japan's Q3 premium is a decade-high $395 (Platts-assessed), China is destocking at a four-year-high pace, and ~92%-Russian-origin LME stock covers ~1.2 days of global output. Against that: a hawkish Fed (10Y 4.59%; Sept hike odds ~69%), DXY >101, US/EU spot premia easing off May peaks, and China's capped-but-exporting supply. Net: constructive above $3,077; conviction upgrades only through $3,260; $3,050 remains the line.

feed.text: Thursday pre-officials check: latest OFFICIAL is Wed 08-Jul — cash $3,141.00 / 3M $3,147.00 / stock 290,825 t (Westmetall), a FOURTH straight up-day. SMM (09-Jul): SHFE night close ¥23,020 (-0.24%); destocking at a four-year-high pace; morning spot eased with deep-discount buying in central China (-¥120–140 vs Jul contract). China June CPI +1.0% (miss) / PPI +4.1% (near-4-yr high) released this morning.

Westmetall posted the 08-Jul OFFICIAL: cash $3,141.00 / 3M $3,147.00 / stock 290,825 t — +0.71/+0.62%, the fourth consecutive gain; contango narrowed to -$6.00. Evening close: doji $3,138.5 (-0.02%) after a $3,125.5–3,165 range — MA10 (~$3,145) is the immediate hurdle.

US STRIKES: dozens of Iranian targets hit in retaliation for the 06–07 Jul ship attacks; Iran's oil-sale waiver revoked; Trump warned Iran would be hit 'very hard' — then framed the exchange as short of a long-term campaign. Indirect technical talks (fund returns, strait security) continue; nuclear consultations near (CBS/ms.now/SMM).

OIL & RATES: Brent settled $78.02 (+5.2%), WTI $73.52 (+4.4%) — the sharpest jump since the war began; US 10Y 4.59% (mid-May high); DXY held >101; FOMC June minutes showed a divided, hawkish committee — cost support for metal, rates headwind for price (CNBC/TE).

PREMIUM MARKS: Platts assessed US MW at 108.7¢/lb (-10.35¢ vs 20-May) and EDU Rotterdam at $525–560 (mid $542.5, -$70 vs 13-May) on 03-Jul; then assessed Q3 MJP at a decade-high $395/t (06-Jul) — regional spot easing off the June spike while Japan's quarterly resets up (S&P Global/IndexBox).

FREIGHT: BDI 2,875 (+2.8%) — highest since 08-Jun; Capesize 4,514, a sixth straight daily gain — dry-bulk legs for bauxite/alumina rising; Gulf war-risk premia re-bid (Baltic Exchange via Baird Maritime).

news.headline: US RETALIATION: after Iran struck three vessels in/near Hormuz (06–07 Jul; laden Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat, Saudi VLCC Wedyan, +1), the US hit dozens of Iranian targets on 08-Jul and revoked Iran's oil-sale waiver; Trump warned Iran would be struck 'very hard' but said the exchange should not become a long-term war. Indirect technical talks — including strait security — continue. Transit continues; the ~700kt H2 Gulf-return supply framing keeps a live two-way discount.
news.source: CBS News / ms.now live blogs (08-Jul); Britannica 2026 Iran-war timeline — cbsnews.com/live-updates/iran-us-war-strait-of-hormuz-trump-nato/

news.headline: FOURTH straight official gain: 08-Jul cash $3,141.00 / 3M $3,147.00 (+0.71/+0.62%), stock 290,825 t (-1,600, below 291kt), contango -$6.00 and narrowing. The evening close was a doji $3,138.5 into MA10 (~$3,145) — $3,260 (.382) remains the upgrade trigger, $3,050 the line.
news.source: Westmetall (08-Jul officials); SMM morning brief (09-Jul) — westmetall.com / metal.com

news.headline: OIL SHOCK MEETS HAWKISH FED: Brent settled +5.2% at $78.02 (08-Jul); US 10Y 4.59% (mid-May high); DXY >101; FOMC minutes divided-hawkish; CME September cumulative-hike odds ~69%. China June CPI missed (+1.0%) while PPI ran +4.1% — the hottest since Jul-2022: energy costs squeezing margins globally.
news.source: CNBC (08/09-Jul); Trading Economics; CME FedWatch via SMM — cnbc.com/2026/07/08/oil-prices-brent-wti-iran-us-hormuz.html

news.headline: DESTOCKING ACCELERATES: ingot stocks 1.078 Mt (Thu), -20kt vs Mon / -52kt w/w; warehouse withdrawals at a four-year high; secondary-alloy ingots -9kt (6th straight weekly draw); liquid-aluminium share rising. SHFE settled +0.68% (¥23,075); A00 ¥23,040 (+100). SMM: one-sided weakness 'difficult to sustain'; import window shut.
news.source: SMM Morning Brief & Aluminum Briefing (08/09-Jul) — metal.com

news.headline: JAPAN DECADE HIGH, WEST EASING: Platts assessed Q3 MJP at $395/t (06-Jul, highest in 10 years; 4th straight quarterly rise); US MW eased to 108.7¢/lb and EDU Rotterdam to $525–560 (both 03-Jul, off May peaks) — the tariff-$ leg deflates with the LME while Japan's structural reset sticks.
news.source: S&P Global Platts via IndexBox (06-Jul); Platts marks (03-Jul) — indexbox.io

news.headline: SUPPLY PATH REPAIRING — SLOWLY AND AT RISK: EGA's Al Taweelah restart progresses (~12-month horizon), the Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG bauxite flows (GAC assets to state-owned Nimba Mining), Metro Mining shipped a record 1.8 Mt in Q2, and China exports run +12–18% — but Mozal (~580ktpa) sits on care & maintenance and every Gulf leg transits a contested corridor.
news.source: Mining.com / Alcircle (Guinea–EGA); Investegate (South32 Mozal); SMM (08-Jul) — mining.com

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio (Boddington, Worsley, MRN, Alumar, South Africa ops) for $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~$1.0bn stock; up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 — making Alcoa the world's largest bauxite miner. Q2 results due 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT).

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due 22-Jul (07:00 CEST).

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 09-Jul (no new releases since the last run). Q2 season dates: Alcoa 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT), Rio Tinto Q2 production report mid-Jul, Norsk Hydro 22-Jul (07:00 CEST), Maaden (1211) late-Jul/early-Aug. Corporate action: Alcoa–South32 bauxite/alumina/aluminium deal — $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~17.0m shares ≈ $1.0bn), up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030; close expected H1-2027 (announced 01-Jul). Context: South32's Mozal smelter (~580ktpa) has been on care & maintenance since 15-Mar-2026. Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Reuters, Bloomberg, BusinessWire, Alcircle, Investegate.

commercial.text: Four official up-days off the .50 retrace ($3,077; 3M now $3,147) with an explicitly two-way Hormuz tape = a firmer bid, not yet a confirmed trend. Keep LME hedges on priced volume; pace unpriced tonnes into strength; a clean daily close through $3,260 (.382) upgrades the count, while $3,050 remains the invalidation line. Do NOT pre-sell the H2 'Gulf-return' surplus narrative — attacks and US strikes traded within 48 hours on that corridor.

Japan has re-based: Q3 MJP $395/t is Platts-assessed (06-Jul), a decade high, and Q4 talks (~Sep) open against that anchor (scenario band $350–425). The West is easing off the spike — Platts US MW 108.7¢/lb and EDU Rotterdam $542.5 mid (03-Jul, both down vs May) — so time US/EU spot commitments carefully while locking Asian quarterly exposure early if Gulf risk persists.

Freight is bid on both risk and demand: BDI 2,875 (+2.8%, six straight Capesize gains) and Gulf war-risk surcharges re-pricing after the attack/strike cycle. Keep war-risk cover in place, hold land-bridge readiness elevated, re-validate carrier capacity weekly, and keep CIF/FOB optionality plus force-majeure language current on Gulf-loading contracts.

The macro ceiling is real: 10Y 4.59%, DXY >101, September cumulative-hike odds ~69%, and China's PPI at +4.1% signals cost-push, not demand-pull. Stress hedge books for a $3,050 break and for a $3,260 breakout; monitor FX legs (USD/CNY ~6.78, USD/JPY ~162.5) on Asian premium realisation.

bottom_line: On 09-Jul the market is running two tapes at once. The official tape is constructive: four straight gains to $3,141 cash / $3,147 3M, stock below 291kt, contango -$6 and narrowing, Japan's Q3 premium a decade-high $395 (Platts) and China destocking at a four-year-high pace — the structural-deficit floor is doing its job above $3,077. The macro tape is hostile: the US just struck dozens of Iranian targets, Brent settled +5.2%, the 10Y sits at 4.59%, the Fed minutes read hawkish and September hike odds are ~69% — a rates/USD ceiling pressing on every rally, visible in Wednesday's doji close at MA10. Playbook unchanged: constructive above $3,077, upgrade only through $3,260 (.382), respect $3,050 — and treat the H2 Gulf-return supply story as discounted, not dead: every leg of it still transits a corridor where attacks and strikes traded within 48 hours.
sources: London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (latest official Wed 08-Jul: Al cash $3,141.00 / 3M $3,147.00 / stock 290,825 t — verified at run time; Thursday officials post ~13:20 London).
Base metals board (Cu/Zn officials; Ni/Pb closes)

forward_path.basis: 08-Jul Westmetall official cash; +0.71% vs 07-Jul — a FOURTH straight up-day, extending the base above the $3,077 (.50) support.

outlook.curve_note: The front end sits in a NARROWING shallow contango (-$6.00 on 08-Jul vs -$8.50) — no acute squeeze, but unbroken stock draws (290,825 t, below 291kt) keep nearby dates supported, and a flip back to backwardation would flag the war-premium regime returning. Consensus 2026 averages sit in the $3,200–3,500 band before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply repairs; ~700kt of Gulf metal is framed to return in H2 and China exports run +12–18% — but that supply path carries a live two-way discount after a 48-hour window in which Iranian ship attacks and US strikes on dozens of targets both occurred, and Platts' decade-high $395 Q3 MJP shows the physical market paying up regardless. The structural deficit remains the floor under the $3,050–3,130 support zone.

ai_analysis: What changed: Tuesday's 07-Jul officials delivered a THIRD straight up-day (Al 3M $3,127.50, +0.47%; stock 292,425 t, -3,125) — and the session absorbed the biggest Gulf-shipping attack day since the US–Iran deal (3 vessels hit in 24h; US sanctions on Iranian oil re-imposed; CENTCOM strikes). The recovery now has both a technical base AND a re-opening geopolitical bid.

Term structure: cash–3M at -$8.50 — still a shallow contango, but the combination of sub-293kt stock, daily draws and renewed corridor risk is the setup from which June's backwardation emerged; watch the front spreads for the first sign of re-tightening.

Drivers: physical tightness is not easing — Q3 MJP settled $395 (SMM spot $380, +123.5% YTD), EU premia >60% above end-2025, China destocking at a four-year-high pace with the import window shut. Against that: EGA ahead of restart plan, ~700kt H2 Gulf return framed, China exports +12–18%, Indonesia ramping — supply that must transit a Strait under fire again.

Positioning read: SMM notes modest bullish position-building on the LME (OI +103 lots into the +0.84% close) and bear-position reduction on the SHFE — consistent with a market covering the 'peace-dividend' short rather than chasing length; a $3,260 (.382) reclaim is the trigger that would force the chase.

premium_explainer: Premiums are the physical-delivery adders over the LME price: they capture regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness, not the metal itself. Japan (MJP) settles quarterly — Q3-2026 is a decade-high $395/t, Platts-assessed 06-Jul — while Europe and the US mark daily/weekly: the fresh 03-Jul Platts marks show both EASING off June's war spike (EDU Rotterdam mid $542.5, -$70 vs mid-May; US MW 108.7¢/lb, -10.35¢ vs 20-May) even as Japan's quarterly resets structurally higher. US values remain tariff-inflated: at the 50% Section-232 rate the tariff-$ component alone is ≈$1,570 at the 08-Jul LME cash — the decomposition below separates policy from market.
premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 settled AND Platts-assessed at $395/t (06-Jul; decade high; fourth consecutive rise: $86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM's end-June spot ($380, +123.5% YTD) confirms convergence. Q4 talks (~Sep) against the $350–425 scenario band: persistent Gulf risk argues the top half, durable de-escalation the bottom.

European (EDU): Platts marked $525–560 (mid $542.5) on 03-Jul — off $70 from mid-May as the panic unwinds — but the 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal (~580ktpa) on care & maintenance keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war; renewed Gulf attacks re-bid freight/war-risk pass-through.

US Midwest: Platts 108.7¢/lb (03-Jul, ≈$2,396) — easing with the LME's tariff-$ leg, not with market tightness; policy is roughly two-thirds of the all-in US premium, keeping US conversion economics hostage to Section 232 (watch derivative-product scope expansions).

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: surplus builds through H2 — expect ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band (FOB Australia ~$340 easing, API ~$304, Platts futures ~$309); SHFE AO (¥2,716) remains below all MAs with port stocks accumulating — rallies are short-covering until Guinea's quota policy lands.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38) — record Q2 Australian shipments (Metro Mining 1.8 Mt, guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held) add length; Guinea's export-curb push (targeting >$100/dmt) is the key upside risk to the cost curve.
Caustic soda / lime: stable-to-firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia caustic); Gulf war-risk surcharges are the main freight-side swing on refining reagents while the strait stays contested.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — Brent settled +5.2% at $78.02 on 08-Jul and EU gas spiked after the LNG-carrier attack: the live risk), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,169.00 08-Jul official, -1.4%).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics has FLIPPED from tailwind to watch-item: Hormuz shipping is under attack again (3 vessels in 24h, 07-Jul) and the BDI is rising (2,797, +2.9%; Capesize +12% w/w) — freight and war-risk assumptions on bauxite/alumina/metal lanes need immediate refresh.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at $3,855 (02-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and the ABC correction that followed looks complete: A $3,359 (16-Jun), B $3,406 (22-Jun), C $3,061.5 (02-Jul), landing inside the $2,950–3,080 confluence (.50 retracement / prior 4th-of-lesser-degree). FOUR consecutive higher officials now stand off that low ($3,087 → $3,113 → $3,127.5 → $3,147) — the longest run since the top — printed against an explicitly two-way tape: Iranian attacks on three vessels, then US strikes on dozens of targets, with technical talks continuing throughout. Base case (60%): the corrective low is in at $3,061.5 and a multi-month recovery leg builds toward $3,400–3,600 while price holds $3,050. Bear alternate (25%): this bounce is a lower-degree (b)-wave and a final probe of the .618 at $2,894 completes a deeper C — the hawkish-Fed channel (10Y 4.59%, DXY >101) is the fuel for that path. Bull alternate (15%): Gulf escalation persists and price accelerates through $3,260 (.382) toward a $3,700+ retest. Invalidation: a weekly close below $2,850 voids the corrective count and re-opens $2,600. Confidence: moderate — improving on the four-day structure, tempered by Wednesday's doji at MA10.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Daily (swing): from the $3,855 top, wave A fell to $3,359 (16-Jun), wave B was sold at the $3,607 spike (22-Jun), and wave C unwound to $3,065 3M / $3,061.5 cash (02-Jul) — a textbook tag of the .50 retracement ($3,077). FOUR consecutive official up-days have followed ($3,087 → $3,113 → $3,127.5 → $3,147), but Wednesday's evening session printed a doji at $3,138.5 directly into MA10 (~$3,145) with a slight short build (+2.1k lots OI) — the tape is testing, not yet breaking, the first shelf. Roadmap: while price holds $3,050 the base case (55%) extends through $3,150–3,165 (Wednesday's high) toward $3,260 (.382), whose clean daily close confirms the correction complete and opens $3,400; a rejection here and a daily close below $3,050 re-opens $2,990–3,020 (30%); a decisive $2,950 break exposes $2,894 (.618) (15%). Momentum agrees with patience: the daily bearish MACD histogram is near exhaustion (-3.7) while the 4-hour holds a golden cross — consistent with an impulse trying to turn but not yet confirmed. Invalidation for the base: a daily close below $3,050. Confidence: moderate-to-firm.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz — two-way risk: ship attacks, then US strikes (06–08 Jul)
premium_drivers.d: Three vessels (incl. laden Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat and Saudi VLCC Wedyan) were struck 06–07 Jul; the US retaliated on 08-Jul with strikes on dozens of Iranian targets and revoked Iran's oil-sale waiver — while indirect technical talks on strait security continue and Washington framed the exchange as short of a long-term campaign. Freight and war-risk premia stay bid; the ~700kt H2 Gulf-return supply assumption still carries a live discount.

premium_drivers.t: Japan Q3 — decade-high settlement, now Platts-assessed
premium_drivers.d: Platts assessed Q3-2026 MJP at $395/t CIF Japan (06-Jul) — the highest in ten years, +12% q/q — following the Rio Tinto/South32 settlements (03-Jul); SMM's end-June spot ($380, +123.5% YTD) confirms spot-to-settlement convergence. Buyers dependent on Middle-East metal are paying structurally more despite the LME's ~18% correction.

The 50% rate on full customs value (eff. 8-Jun-2026) keeps the US MW structurally inflated — but the fresh Platts mark eased to 108.7¢/lb (03-Jul, -10.35¢ vs 20-May) because the tariff-$ leg deflates mechanically with the LME (≈$1,570 at the 08-Jul cash $3,141). Policy, not physical tightness, is roughly two-thirds of the US all-in premium.

Platts EDU Rotterdam eased to $525–560 (03-Jul) as June's panic unwound, but the 6% EU duty (reverted 1-Jan-2026), CBAM carbon costs and South32's Mozal smelter (~580ktpa) sitting on care & maintenance since 15-Mar-2026 (Cahora Bassa drought/power impasse) keep European supply structurally tighter than pre-war.

so_what.line: Two-way risk is now the tape: the US struck dozens of Iranian targets hours after the Gulf tanker attacks and Brent settled +5.2%, yet aluminium ground out a FOURTH straight official gain (cash $3,141 / 3M $3,147, stock below 291kt) while the evening close printed a flat doji — the market is pricing escalation and de-escalation at once, and $3,260 remains the deciding level.
points: Exchange: Wednesday's officials rose again — cash $3,141 (+0.71%) / 3M $3,147 (+0.62%), a FOURTH consecutive up-day off the $3,077 base; the contango narrowed to -$6 and LME stock fell to 290,825 t. But the evening close was a doji at $3,138.5 into MA10 (~$3,145): the rally needs $3,260 (.382) to upgrade, and $3,050 re-opens the downside.

Geopolitics & macro: the US hit dozens of Iranian targets on 08-Jul in retaliation for the ship attacks and revoked Iran's oil-sale waiver — yet indirect technical talks on strait security continue. Brent settled $78.02 (+5.2%), the US 10Y jumped to 4.59% (highest since mid-May), DXY held above 101 and the FOMC minutes read divided-hawkish: a rates ceiling pressing on a supply-risk floor.

China: June CPI missed at +1.0% y/y while PPI accelerated to +4.1% (hottest since Jul-2022, energy-driven) — demand soft, costs hot. Physically, destocking runs at a four-year-high pace (ingot stocks 1.078 Mt, -52kt w/w) and SHFE settled +0.68% at ¥23,075 — China is a support, not a drag, and the import window stays shut.

Watch: whether officials clear MA10 and press toward $3,260 vs a $3,050 break; Q4 MJP talks against Platts' decade-high $395 Q3 anchor (US MW 108.7¢ and EDU Rotterdam $542.5 both easing off May peaks); Alcoa's Q2 on 16-Jul; and September FOMC odds (~69% cumulative-hike) as the macro swing.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
